logo for the NFL franchise St. Louis Rams.

The [2009 season](/wiki/2009_NFL_season "2009 NFL season") was the [St. Louis Rams](/wiki/St._Louis_Rams "St. Louis Rams")' 72nd in the [National Football League](/wiki/National_Football_League "National Football League") (NFL), and their 15th in [St. Louis](/wiki/St._Louis "St. Louis"). Due to the Rams winning only against the [Detroit Lions](/wiki/2009_Detroit_Lions_season "2009 Detroit Lions season"), they finished with the NFL's worst record at 1–15, earning the first overall selection in the [2010 NFL draft](/wiki/2010_NFL_draft "2010 NFL draft") in [Steve Spagnuolo](/wiki/Steve_Spagnuolo "Steve Spagnuolo")'s first season as a head coach.

The 2009 Rams scored 175 points (10.9 per game), the sixth fewest for a 16-game NFL season.[[1]](#cite_note-1) The Rams threw only twelve combined touchdown passes all season – including one by [kicker](/wiki/Placekicker "Placekicker") [Josh Brown](/wiki/Josh_Brown_(American_football) "Josh Brown (American football)").[[2]](#cite_note-2) Quarterback [Marc Bulger](/wiki/Marc_Bulger "Marc Bulger") led the team with five touchdown passes. *[Pro Football Reference](/wiki/Pro-Football-Reference.com "Pro-Football-Reference.com")* argues that the 2009 Rams[[3]](#cite_note-3) were the worst team fielded in the NFL since the expansion Tampa Bay Buccaneers of [1976](/wiki/1976_Tampa_Bay_Buccaneers_season "1976 Tampa Bay Buccaneers season") and [1977](/wiki/1977_Tampa_Bay_Buccaneers_season "1977 Tampa Bay Buccaneers season"), having a much weaker schedule than the winless 2008 Lions.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

In the three seasons from [2007](/wiki/2007_St._Louis_Rams_season "2007 St. Louis Rams season") to 2009, the Rams won a total of six games, this being the worst record over such a period since [the Chicago Cardinals](/wiki/List_of_Arizona_Cardinals_seasons "List of Arizona Cardinals seasons") during World War II until the [2015](/wiki/2015_Cleveland_Browns_season "2015 Cleveland Browns season") to [2017](/wiki/2017_Cleveland_Browns_season "2017 Cleveland Browns season") Cleveland Browns won only four games.

This was the first season since 1998 that [Torry Holt](/wiki/Torry_Holt "Torry Holt") was not on the opening day roster.

## Offseason

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2009_St._Louis_Rams_season&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Offseason")]

### Player movement

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2009_St._Louis_Rams_season&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Player movement")]

The Rams announced that [Jim Haslett](/wiki/Jim_Haslett "Jim Haslett") would not be returning as head coach after a disastrous 2–14 finish to the 2008 season.[[5]](#cite_note-5) On January 16, [New York Giants](/wiki/2008_New_York_Giants_season "2008 New York Giants season")’ defensive coordinator [Steve Spagnuolo](/wiki/Steve_Spagnuolo "Steve Spagnuolo") signed a four-year, $11.5 million deal to be their new head coach.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

[Jason Brown](/wiki/Jason_Brown_(American_football) "Jason Brown (American football)"), a [center](/wiki/Center_(American_football) "Center (American football)") who formerly played for [the Baltimore Ravens](/wiki/2008_Baltimore_Ravens_season "2008 Baltimore Ravens season") before becoming a [free agent](/wiki/Free_agent "Free agent"), was signed on February 27, 2009, to a five-year deal to bolster the offensive line.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

[Ron Bartell](/wiki/Ron_Bartell "Ron Bartell"), a [cornerback](/wiki/Cornerback "Cornerback"), signed a four-year deal on March 2, worth $13.6 million. Guaranteed, with incentives it could be as much as $28 million.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

[James Butler](/wiki/James_Butler_(American_football) "James Butler (American football)"), a [safety](/wiki/Safety_(American_football_position) "Safety (American football position)"), signed a four-year deal on March 10, that with incentives could be worth almost $17 million.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

[Orlando Pace](/wiki/Orlando_Pace "Orlando Pace"), the premier [offensive tackle](/wiki/Offensive_tackle "Offensive tackle") in the NFL, was released by the Rams to save six million dollars in [salary cap](/wiki/Salary_cap "Salary cap") space.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

On March 13, [wide receiver](/wiki/Wide_receiver "Wide receiver") [Torry Holt](/wiki/Torry_Holt "Torry Holt") was released after ten seasons with the Rams.[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12) Holt was later signed by the [Jacksonville Jaguars](/wiki/Jacksonville_Jaguars "Jacksonville Jaguars").

On May 8, [Pisa Tinoisamoa](/wiki/Pisa_Tinoisamoa "Pisa Tinoisamoa"), the Rams’ leading tackler in [2008](/wiki/2008_St._Louis_Rams_season "2008 St. Louis Rams season"), was given his release.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

## Regular season summaries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2009_St._Louis_Rams_season&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Regular season summaries")]

### Week 1: at Seattle Seahawks

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2009_St._Louis_Rams_season&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Week 1: at Seattle Seahawks")]

| Quarter | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | Total |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |

| **Seahawks** | 0 | 14 | 14 | 0 | **28** |

*at [Qwest Field](/wiki/Qwest_Field "Qwest Field"), [Seattle](/wiki/Seattle "Seattle")*

* **Game time**: 4:15 pm [EDT](/wiki/Eastern_Daylight_Time "Eastern Daylight Time")
* **Game weather**: 73 °F or 22.8 °C (Sunny)
* **Game attendance**: 67,610
* **Referee**: [Pete Morelli](/wiki/Pete_Morelli "Pete Morelli")
* **TV announcers ([Fox](/wiki/NFL_on_Fox "NFL on Fox"))**: [Ron Pitts](/wiki/Ron_Pitts "Ron Pitts"), [John Lynch](/wiki/John_Lynch_(American_football) "John Lynch (American football)"), and [Rob Desir](/w/index.php?title=Rob_Desir&action=edit&redlink=1 "Rob Desir (page does not exist)")

The Rams began their season at [Qwest Field](/wiki/Qwest_Field "Qwest Field") with a Week 1 divisional duel with their [NFC West](/wiki/NFC_West "NFC West") rival, [the Seattle Seahawks](/wiki/2009_Seattle_Seahawks_season "2009 Seattle Seahawks season"). After a scoreless first quarter, St. Louis trailed in the second quarter as Seahawks quarterback [Matt Hasselbeck](/wiki/Matt_Hasselbeck "Matt Hasselbeck") completed a 1-yard touchdown pass to tight end [John Carlson](/wiki/John_Carlson_(American_football) "John Carlson (American football)"). The Rams appeared to have a game-tying touchdown after blocking a Seattle field goal by Seahawks kicker [Olindo Mare](/wiki/Olindo_Mare "Olindo Mare") late in the quarter. However, it was overturned because there were twelve Rams’ players on the field, leading to a Seahawks 12-yard touchdown pass to wide receiver [Nate Burleson](/wiki/Nate_Burleson "Nate Burleson"). In the third quarter, Seattle continued its dominance with Hasselbeck completing a 33-yard touchdown pass to Carlson, along with running back [Julius Jones](/wiki/Julius_Jones_(American_football) "Julius Jones (American football)") getting a 62-yard touchdown run. From there on out, the Seahawks’ defense helped secure a shutout win.

With this loss, the Rams began their season at 0–1.

* **TV announcers ([Fox](/wiki/NFL_on_Fox "NFL on Fox"))**: [Sam Rosen](/wiki/Sam_Rosen_(sportscaster) "Sam Rosen (sportscaster)") and [Tim Ryan](/wiki/Tim_Ryan_(American_football,_born_1967) "Tim Ryan (American football, born 1967)")

Hoping to rebound from their shutout loss to the Seahawks, the Rams flew to [FedEx Field](/wiki/FedExField "FedExField") for a Week 2 duel with [the Washington Redskins](/wiki/2009_Washington_Redskins_season "2009 Washington Redskins season"). St. Louis trailed early as Redskins kicker [Shaun Suisham](/wiki/Shaun_Suisham "Shaun Suisham") got a 21-yard field goal. In the second quarter, the Rams’ deficit increased a Suisham made a 28-yard field goal. Afterwards, St. Louis took the lead as quarterback [Marc Bulger](/wiki/Marc_Bulger "Marc Bulger") completed a 2-yard touchdown pass to wide receiver [Laurent Robinson](/wiki/Laurent_Robinson "Laurent Robinson"). However, Washington got their lead again as Suisham nailed a 23-yard field goal. From there on out, the Rams were unable to obtain further points.

With this loss, St. Louis fell to 0–2.

### Week 3: vs. Green Bay Packers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2009_St._Louis_Rams_season&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Week 3: vs. Green Bay Packers")]

| Quarter | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | Total |

| Rams | 0 | 14 | 3 | 0 | **17** |

*at [Edward Jones Dome](/wiki/Edward_Jones_Dome "Edward Jones Dome"), [St. Louis, Missouri](/wiki/St._Louis,_Missouri "St. Louis, Missouri")*

* **Game time**: 1:00 p.m. [EDT](/wiki/Eastern_Daylight_Time "Eastern Daylight Time")/12:00 p.m. [CDT](/wiki/Central_Daylight_Time "Central Daylight Time")
* **Game weather**: None (Domed Stadium)
* **Game attendance**: 60,234
* **Referee**: [Walt Coleman](/wiki/Walt_Coleman "Walt Coleman")
* **TV announcers ([Fox](/wiki/NFL_on_Fox "NFL on Fox"))**: [Chris Myers](/wiki/Chris_Myers "Chris Myers") and [Trent Green](/wiki/Trent_Green "Trent Green")

Still searching for their first win of the season, the Rams played their Week 3 home opener against [the Green Bay Packers](/wiki/2009_Green_Bay_Packers_season "2009 Green Bay Packers season"). St. Louis immediately trailed early in the first quarter as Packers kicker [Mason Crosby](/wiki/Mason_Crosby "Mason Crosby") got a 48, a 38, and a 25-yard field goal. Green Bay would increase their lead in the second quarter as fullback [John Kuhn](/wiki/John_Kuhn "John Kuhn") got a 1-yard touchdown run. The Rams would respond with the arm of quarterback [Kyle Boller](/wiki/Kyle_Boller "Kyle Boller"), as he hooked up with tight end [Daniel Fells](/wiki/Daniel_Fells "Daniel Fells") on a 16-yard touchdown pass. However, the Packers would strike back with quarterback [Aaron Rodgers](/wiki/Aaron_Rodgers "Aaron Rodgers")'s 21-yard touchdown pass to wide receiver [Donald Driver](/wiki/Donald_Driver "Donald Driver"). St. Louis would close out the half with Boller and Fells hooking up with each other again on a 19-yard touchdown pass.

The Rams would inch closer in the third quarter with kicker [Josh Brown](/wiki/Josh_Brown_(American_football) "Josh Brown (American football)")'s 53-yard field goal. However, Green Bay would pull away in the fourth quarter as Rodgers got a 4-yard touchdown run (with a failed PAT) and completed a 10-yard touchdown pass to Kuhn.

With this loss, St. Louis fell to 0–3.

Starting quarterback [Marc Bulger](/wiki/Marc_Bulger "Marc Bulger") (3-of-4, 23 yards) left the game in the second quarter with an injury to his right shoulder.

Following their home loss to the Packers, the Rams flew to [Candlestick Park](/wiki/Candlestick_Park "Candlestick Park") for a Week 4 NFC West duel with their hated rival, [the San Francisco 49ers](/wiki/2009_San_Francisco_49ers_season "2009 San Francisco 49ers season"). Due to an injury to [Marc Bulger](/wiki/Marc_Bulger "Marc Bulger"), quarterback [Kyle Boller](/wiki/Kyle_Boller "Kyle Boller") got the start.

After a scoreless first quarter, St. Louis’ seasonal struggles continued on the 49ers’ second punt of the game. The ball would hit backup cornerback [Quincy Butler](/wiki/Quincy_Butler_(American_football) "Quincy Butler (American football)")'s foot and roll into the endzone where backup Niners linebacker [Scott McKillop](/wiki/Scott_McKillop "Scott McKillop") would land on it for a touchdown. San Francisco would continue its dominating run in the third quarter with quarterback [Shaun Hill](/wiki/Shaun_Hill "Shaun Hill") hooking up on a 13-yard touchdown pass to tight end [Vernon Davis](/wiki/Vernon_Davis "Vernon Davis"), followed by linebacker [Patrick Willis](/wiki/Patrick_Willis "Patrick Willis") returning an interception 23 yards for a touchdown. The 49ers would wrap up their victory march in the fourth quarter with Hill finding wide receiver [Josh Morgan](/wiki/Josh_Morgan "Josh Morgan") on a 24-yard touchdown pass, followed by defensive tackle [Ray McDonald](/wiki/Ray_McDonald "Ray McDonald") returning a fumble 11 yards for a touchdown.

With their second shutout loss of the season, the Rams fell to 0–4.

Hoping to get their first win of the season, the Rams went home, donned their throwback uniforms, and prepared for a Week 5 duel with [the Minnesota Vikings](/wiki/2009_Minnesota_Vikings_season "2009 Minnesota Vikings season"). Even though quarterback [Marc Bulger](/wiki/Marc_Bulger "Marc Bulger") was almost back to 100% health, backup quarterback [Kyle Boller](/wiki/Kyle_Boller "Kyle Boller") got the start.

In the first quarter, St. Louis continued to struggle as Vikings running back [Adrian Peterson](/wiki/Adrian_Peterson "Adrian Peterson") got a 5-yard touchdown run and defensive end [Jared Allen](/wiki/Jared_Allen "Jared Allen") returned a fumble 52 yards for a touchdown. The Rams would get on the board in the second quarter with a 29-yard field goal from kicker [Josh Brown](/wiki/Josh_Brown_(American_football) "Josh Brown (American football)"), but Minnesota came right back with kicking [Ryan Longwell](/wiki/Ryan_Longwell "Ryan Longwell") nailing a 47-yard field goal.

The Vikings would continue to make their presence felt in the third quarter with quarterback [Brett Favre](/wiki/Brett_Favre "Brett Favre") completing a 13-yard touchdown to tight end [Visanthe Shiancoe](/wiki/Visanthe_Shiancoe "Visanthe Shiancoe"). Minnesota would follow it up in the fourth quarter with Peterson's 7-yard touchdown. St. Louis tried to rally as quarterback [Marc Bulger](/wiki/Marc_Bulger "Marc Bulger") completed a 27-yard touchdown pass to wide receiver [Donnie Avery](/wiki/Donnie_Avery "Donnie Avery"), but the Vikings would close out the game with running back [Chester Taylor](/wiki/Chester_Taylor "Chester Taylor")'s 1-yard touchdown run.

With this loss, the Rams fell to 0–5.

Still on the hunt for their first win of the season, the Rams flew to [Jacksonville Municipal Stadium](/wiki/Jacksonville_Municipal_Stadium "Jacksonville Municipal Stadium") for a Week 6 interconference duel with [the Jacksonville Jaguars](/wiki/2009_Jacksonville_Jaguars_season "2009 Jacksonville Jaguars season"). St. Louis got the opening charge in the first quarter with quarterback [Marc Bulger](/wiki/Marc_Bulger "Marc Bulger")'s 17-yard touchdown pass to wide receiver [Donnie Avery](/wiki/Donnie_Avery "Donnie Avery"). The Jaguars would answer with running back [Maurice Jones-Drew](/wiki/Maurice_Jones-Drew "Maurice Jones-Drew") getting a 4-yard touchdown run (with a failed PAT). Afterwards, the Rams would get the only points of the second quarter as kicker [Josh Brown](/wiki/Josh_Brown_(American_football) "Josh Brown (American football)") nailed a 52-yard field goal.

After a scoreless third quarter, a back-and-forth quarter would ensue. Jones-Drew would help Jacksonville take the lead with a 1-yard touchdown run, yet Rams defensive end [Leonard Little](/wiki/Leonard_Little "Leonard Little") would return an interception 36 yards for a touchdown. Following a 3-yard touchdown run by Jones-Drew, St. Louis would tie the game with Brown's 27-yard field goal. However, in overtime, the Jaguars got the last laugh as kicker [Josh Scobee](/wiki/Josh_Scobee "Josh Scobee") booted the game-winning 36-yard field goal.

With this loss, the Rams fell to 0–6.

## Local television blackouts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2009_St._Louis_Rams_season&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Local television blackouts")]

In the 2009 season, the Rams had three regular season games blacked out on local television. After selling out their first five games, attendance began to drop and the Rams couldn't sell out their final three games against [the Seahawks](/wiki/2009_Seattle_Seahawks_season "2009 Seattle Seahawks season"), [Texans](/wiki/2009_Houston_Texans_season "2009 Houston Texans season"), and [49ers](/wiki/2009_San_Francisco_49ers_season "2009 San Francisco 49ers season"). In all of these instances, the Rams fell well short of a sellout and did not even ask for a deadline extension from the NFL to try to sell remaining tickets and avert a blackout. Since 2007, the Rams had only won two games at the [Edward Jones Dome](/wiki/Edward_Jones_Dome "Edward Jones Dome").
